title: Differentiation, Integration, and Linear Algebra Refresher

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: Apply the power rule to each term; constants differentiate to zero.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: The power rule also applies to negative and fractional exponents.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: Rewrite quotients as negative powers.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: Convert the radical to a fractional exponent.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: Both \(\pi\) and \(e^2\) are constants.

questions.question: Find the tangent line at \(x=2\).
hints: Find both the point on the curve and the derivative at that point.

questions.question: Find all points where the graph has a horizontal tangent.
solutionSteps: Evaluate the function to obtain \(f(-1)=9\) and \(f(3)=-23\).
hints: Horizontal tangents occur where the derivative is zero.

questions.question: Determine where the function is increasing and decreasing.
hints: Use the sign of the first derivative.

questions.question: Find the critical points.
solutionSteps: Set the derivative equal to zero.
hints: Factor the derivative completely.

questions.question: Use the derivative definition to find \(f'(x)\).
solutionSteps: Divide by \(h\) and take the limit to get \(2x+3\).
hints: Expand \(f(x+h)\), cancel, and factor out \(h\).

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: Use the product rule before simplifying.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: Use both the product rule and chain rule.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: Apply the quotient rule carefully.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: A common denominator in the numerator helps simplify.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: Differentiate the outer function, then the inner function.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Apply the chain rule and simplify.
hints: Treat the square root as a one-half power.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Apply the chain rule to the fourth power.
hints: Differentiate the inner quotient first.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Factor and combine over a common denominator.
hints: Factor \(2x(x^3+1)^{-3}\) from the result.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: Factor \(2\) from the derivative of the inner function.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: Factor before fully expanding.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: Multiply by the derivative of the exponent.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Apply the chain rule to the exponent \(x^2-4x\).
hints: The derivative of the exponent is \(2x-4\).

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: Use the product rule and then factor.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Use the standard derivative of the natural logarithm.
hints: Recall \((\ln x)'=1/x\).

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: Differentiate the inside and divide by the inside.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: Apply the product rule.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: Use logarithm quotient properties first.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: For a non-\(e\) base, include \(\ln a\).

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Let \(y=x^x\) and take logarithms: \(\ln y=x\ln x\).
hints: Take natural logarithms before differentiating.

questions.question: Use logarithmic differentiation.
hints: Turn products, quotients, and powers into sums using logarithms.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Use \((\sin x)'=\cos x\) and \((\cos x)'=-\sin x\).
hints: Watch the two negative signs in the cosine term.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: Use the chain rule.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Differentiate the outer sine, then multiply by \(2x\).
hints: Apply the chain rule.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: Differentiate both factors using the product rule.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Apply the chain rule twice and simplify.
hints: There is an outer square and an inner linear function.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: Rewrite as \((\sin x)^3\) and apply the chain rule.
hints: Differentiate the outer cube first.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
solutionSteps: The numerator simplifies to \(-\sin x(1+\sin x)-\cos^2x=-(1+\sin x)\).
hints: Use \(\sin^2x+\cos^2x=1\) when simplifying.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: Here \(u=4x\).

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: Square the entire inner function in the denominator.

questions.question: Differentiate the function.
hints: Apply the product rule and remember the negative derivative of arccosine.

questions.question: Find \(\frac{dy}{dx}\).
hints: Differentiate \(y^2\) using the chain rule.

questions.question: Find the slope at \((1,2)\).
hints: Remember that \((xy)'=x y'+y\).

questions.question: Find \(\frac{dy}{dx}\).
hints: Move all terms containing \(y'\) to one side.

questions.question: Find \(\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}\).
hints: Substitute the original equation near the end.

questions.question: A circle's radius increases at \(3\text{ cm/s}\). How fast is its area increasing when \(r=8\text{ cm}\)?
hints: Differentiate with respect to time.

questions.question: A 10-meter ladder slides down a wall. The bottom moves away at \(2\text{ m/s}\). How fast is the top moving when the bottom is 6 meters from the wall?
solutionSteps: Substitute the known values and solve.
hints: Use the Pythagorean relationship and note that downward velocity is negative.

questions.question: Find the local extrema.
solutionSteps: Use \(f''(x)=6x-12\) to classify the critical points.
hints: Use the second derivative test.

questions.question: Find the absolute maximum and minimum on \([-2,3]\).
hints: Check both critical points and endpoints.

questions.question: Determine intervals of concavity and inflection points.
solutionSteps: Make a sign chart around \(x=0\) and \(x=2\).
Evaluate the function at both sign-change locations.
hints: Concavity is determined by the sign of the second derivative.

questions.question: A rectangle has perimeter 40. Find the dimensions that maximize its area.
hints: Express the area using one variable.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
hints: Increase each power by one and divide by the new power.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Apply the power rule to each term.
hints: Use the integration power rule for both negative and fractional exponents.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
hints: The \(1/x\) case is the logarithmic exception to the power rule.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Divide by the derivative \(2\) of the exponent.
hints: Differentiate your result to check the factor of \(1/2\).

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
hints: For base \(a\neq e\), divide by \(\ln a\).

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
hints: Check signs by differentiating the cosine term.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
hints: Recall the derivative of tangent.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
hints: Recall \((\arctan x)'=1/(1+x^2)\).

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
hints: Recall the derivative of \(\arcsin x\).

questions.question: Find \(F(x)\) given the derivative and initial value.
hints: Integrate first, then use the initial condition.

questions.question: Evaluate the definite integral.
hints: Use the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

questions.question: Evaluate the definite integral.
solutionSteps: The odd term \(x^3\) integrates to zero on \([-1,1]\).
Evaluate the remaining even terms to get \(\frac43-8=-\frac{20}{3}\).
hints: Use symmetry to simplify the odd term.

questions.question: Evaluate the definite integral.
hints: Evaluate the antiderivative at both endpoints.

questions.question: Evaluate the definite integral.
solutionSteps: Integrate from \(0\) to \(\pi/2\); the sine term vanishes.
hints: Use the cosine power-reduction identity.

questions.question: Evaluate the definite integral.
hints: The antiderivative is \(\ln x\) on this positive interval.

questions.question: Find the average value on \([0,3]\).
solutionSteps: The integral is \(9\), so the average is \(3\).
hints: Divide the definite integral by the interval length.

questions.question: Find the area between the curves on \([0,1]\).
hints: Integrate top curve minus bottom curve.

questions.question: Find the total area between the curve and the \(x\)-axis on \([-3,3]\).
solutionSteps: Use symmetry and split where the function changes sign.
hints: Total area uses absolute value, so split at the intercepts.

questions.question: Find \(F'(x)\).
solutionSteps: Apply the Fundamental Theorem with upper limit \(x^2\).
Evaluate the integrand at \(t=x^2\) and multiply by \(2x\).
hints: Use the chain rule for the variable upper limit.

questions.question: Find \(G'(x)\).
hints: Differentiate both variable limits.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
hints: The derivative of the inner expression is already present.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
hints: The numerator is the derivative of the denominator.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
hints: Reverse the chain rule.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Integrate sine and include the factor \(1/5\).
hints: Differentiate the proposed result to check the sign.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
hints: Use the factor \(\sin x\,dx\) for substitution.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
hints: The derivative of \(\ln x\) is \(1/x\).

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
hints: Rewrite the denominator using a negative exponent after substitution.

questions.question: Evaluate the definite integral.
hints: Change the limits when using substitution in a definite integral.

questions.question: Evaluate the definite integral.
hints: Use \(u=\tan x\).

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
hints: Let the algebraic factor be \(u\).

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Then \(v=-\cos x\) and apply integration by parts.
hints: Take \(u=x\) so its derivative becomes simpler.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Apply integration by parts and simplify.
hints: Choose the logarithm as \(u\).

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: After the second application, the original integral reappears.
hints: Two rounds of integration by parts are needed.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
hints: Treat the integrand as a product with 1.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
hints: Use a power-reduction identity.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
hints: Save one sine factor and convert the rest using \(1-\cos^2x\).

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
hints: Save one cosine factor because its power is odd.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
hints: Use the Pythagorean identity for tangent and secant.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
solutionSteps: Use integration by parts with \(u=\sec x\) and \(dv=\sec^2x\,dx\).
The original integral reappears; solve for it.
hints: This standard integral requires integration by parts.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
hints: Factor the denominator first.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
hints: Use partial fractions after factoring the quadratic.

questions.question: Evaluate the indefinite integral.
hints: Use one partial-fraction term for each linear factor.

questions.question: Determine convergence and evaluate if convergent.
hints: Use a limit at the infinite endpoint.

questions.question: Determine whether the integral converges.
hints: The \(p\)-integral with \(p=1\) diverges.

questions.question: Evaluate the improper integral.
solutionSteps: Write the lower endpoint as a limit: \(\lim_{a\to0^+}\int_a^1x^{-1/2}\,dx\).
hints: The integrand is unbounded at zero, so use a limit.

questions.question: Find the volume formed by rotating the region under the curve on \([0,2]\) about the \(x\)-axis.
hints: The disk radius is \(y=x^2\).

questions.question: Find the volume formed by rotating the region bounded by the curves about the \(y\)-axis.
hints: Shell radius is \(x\), and shell height is \(x-x^2\).

questions.question: Find the arc length on \([0,3]\).
hints: Start with the arc-length formula.

questions.question: Find displacement and total distance traveled from \(t=0\) to \(t=5\).
solutionSteps: Sum the absolute changes on \([0,1]\), \([1,3]\), and \([3,5]\) to get \(28/3\).
hints: For total distance, split at every zero of velocity.

questions.question: Compute the vector sum.
hints: Add vectors component by component.

questions.question: Compute the vector expression.
solutionSteps: Scale the vectors to obtain \([3,-6,15]^T\) and \([8,0,-2]^T\).
hints: Perform scalar multiplication before subtraction.

questions.question: Find the magnitude of the vector.
hints: Square each component, add, and take the square root.

questions.question: Find a unit vector in the given direction.
hints: A unit vector is \(\mathbf v/\|\mathbf v\|\).

questions.question: Compute the dot product.
hints: Multiply corresponding components and add.

questions.question: Find the angle between the vectors.
solutionSteps: \(\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v=1\), and both norms are \(\sqrt2\).
hints: Use \(\cos\theta=(\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v)/(\|\mathbf u\|\|\mathbf v\|)\).

questions.question: Determine whether the vectors are parallel.
questions.answer: \text{Parallel; the second vector is }-2\text{ times the first}
hints: Check whether one vector is a scalar multiple of the other.

questions.question: Find the projection of \(\mathbf u\) onto \(\mathbf v\).
solutionSteps: Use \(\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf v}\mathbf u=\frac{\mathbf u\cdot\mathbf v}{\mathbf v\cdot\mathbf v}\mathbf v\).
hints: Use the projection formula.

questions.question: Find a vector orthogonal to both vectors.
solutionSteps: Compute the cross product \(\mathbf u\times\mathbf v\).
The result is \((2,1,-1)\), which has zero dot product with each original vector.
hints: Use the cross product in \(\mathbb R^3\).

questions.question: Find the area of the parallelogram spanned by the vectors.
hints: Parallelogram area equals the norm of the cross product.

questions.question: Solve the system.
hints: Elimination is especially quick here.

questions.question: Solve the system.
solutionSteps: Adding the first two equations gives \(3x+y+2z=12\).
The third equation requires the same left side to equal 10.
This contradiction means the system is inconsistent.
hints: Compare the third equation with the sum of the first two.

questions.question: Row-reduce the augmented matrix.
solutionSteps: Apply elementary row operations to create pivots.
The final row represents \(0=1\), so the corresponding system is inconsistent.
hints: A pivot in the augmented column signals a contradiction.

questions.question: Classify the system.
solutionSteps: The first and third equations have identical left sides but different constants.
hints: Look for contradictory equations before row-reducing.

questions.question: Find all values of \(a\) for which the system has a unique solution.
hints: A square system has a unique solution when its coefficient determinant is nonzero.

questions.question: Express the solution set in parametric vector form.
hints: Choose the free variables as parameters.

questions.question: Determine whether \(\mathbf b\) lies in the given span.
solutionSteps: The first two components give \(c_1=4\) and \(c_2=1\).
hints: Solve for the coefficients using the easiest components first.

questions.question: Find a basis for the solution space.
solutionSteps: Write the general solution as a linear combination of two vectors.
hints: Use the free variables to generate basis vectors.

questions.question: Find the rank and nullity.
solutionSteps: Row reduction produces two pivot rows.
With three columns, rank-nullity gives nullity \(3-2=1\).
hints: Count pivots, then use rank plus nullity equals the number of columns.

questions.question: Determine whether the vectors are linearly independent.
solutionSteps: The second vector is \(2\) times the first.
Therefore a nontrivial linear relation exists.
hints: A set is dependent if one vector is a scalar multiple of another.

questions.question: Compute the matrix sum.
hints: Matrix addition is entrywise.

questions.question: Compute the matrix product.
solutionSteps: Take each row of the first matrix dotted with each column of the second matrix.
hints: Use row-by-column multiplication.

questions.question: Compute \(AB\) and \(BA\).
solutionSteps: Multiply row by column to find \(AB\).
Reverse the order and repeat for \(BA\).
The results differ, illustrating that matrix multiplication is not generally commutative.
hints: Keep the multiplication order explicit.

questions.question: Compute \(A^2\).
hints: Matrix squaring means matrix multiplication, not entrywise squaring.

questions.question: Find the transpose.
solutionSteps: Turn the rows of \(A\) into the columns of \(A^T\).
hints: Swap row and column indices.

questions.question: Find the inverse.
hints: Use the \(2\times2\) inverse formula.

questions.question: Use a matrix inverse to solve the system.
hints: Solve \(\mathbf x=A^{-1}\mathbf b\).

questions.question: Determine whether the matrix is invertible.
solutionSteps: A matrix with zero determinant is singular.
hints: Check the determinant.

questions.question: Solve for \(X\) in \(AX=B\).
hints: Preserve multiplication order when isolating \(X\).

questions.question: Verify \((AB)^{-1}=B^{-1}A^{-1}\).
solutionSteps: Compute \(A^{-1}\) and \(B^{-1}\), then multiply in the order \(B^{-1}A^{-1}\).
hints: The inverse reverses the multiplication order.

questions.question: Compute the determinant.
hints: For a \(2\times2\) matrix, use \(ad-bc\).

questions.question: Compute the determinant.
solutionSteps: Expand along the first row or use row reduction.
hints: Choose a convenient determinant method.

questions.question: Use cofactor expansion to compute the determinant.
hints: The zero in the first row eliminates one cofactor term.

questions.question: Find all values of \(k\) for which the matrix is singular.
solutionSteps: Set the determinant \(k^2-6\) equal to zero.
hints: Singular means determinant zero.

questions.question: Determine whether the polynomials are linearly independent in \(P_2\).
solutionSteps: Represent the polynomials by coefficient vectors \((1,1,0)\), \((0,1,1)\), and \((1,0,1)\).
The determinant of the coefficient matrix is nonzero.
Therefore the set is linearly independent.
hints: Convert each polynomial to its coefficient vector.

questions.question: Find a basis and the dimension of the span.
solutionSteps: The third vector equals the sum of the first two.
The first two are not scalar multiples, so they are independent.
hints: Remove vectors that are combinations of earlier vectors.

questions.question: Find bases for the column space and null space.
solutionSteps: Row reduction shows pivots in columns 1 and 3.
Use the corresponding original columns for a column-space basis.
hints: Use pivot columns from the original matrix, not the reduced matrix.

questions.question: Find the standard matrix of the transformation.
solutionSteps: Use these vectors as the matrix columns.
hints: The standard matrix columns are the images of the standard basis vectors.

questions.question: Determine the kernel and range.
solutionSteps: For the kernel, solve \(x+y=0\) and \(y+z=0\), giving \((-y,y,-y)\).
The transformation matrix has two pivot rows, so its range is all of \(\mathbb R^2\).
hints: Solve \(T(\mathbf x)=\mathbf0\) for the kernel.

questions.question: Determine whether the transformation is linear.
hints: Check what the transformation does to the zero vector.

questions.question: Find the eigenvalues.
solutionSteps: For a diagonal matrix, the eigenvalues are its diagonal entries.
hints: Read the eigenvalues from the diagonal.

questions.question: Find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors.
solutionSteps: Solve each null-space equation to obtain the eigenvectors.
hints: Factor the characteristic polynomial as \((\lambda-3)(\lambda-1)\).

questions.question: Find the characteristic polynomial.
hints: Use \(p(\lambda)=\det(\lambda I-A)\).

questions.question: Determine whether the matrix is diagonalizable.
solutionSteps: The only eigenvalue is \(4\), with algebraic multiplicity 2.
Solving \((A-4I)\mathbf v=0\) gives a one-dimensional eigenspace.
There are not enough independent eigenvectors.
hints: Compare algebraic and geometric multiplicities.

questions.question: Diagonalize the matrix if possible.
solutionSteps: Choose the standard basis eigenvectors, so \(P=I\) and \(D=A\).
hints: A diagonal matrix is already diagonalized.

questions.question: Use diagonalization to compute \(A^5\).
solutionSteps: The eigenvalues are \(3\) and \(1\), with eigenvectors \((1,1)\) and \((1,-1)\).
hints: Raise the diagonal eigenvalue matrix to the fifth power.

questions.question: Apply the Gram-Schmidt process.
solutionSteps: Subtract the projection of \(\mathbf v_2\) onto \(\mathbf v_1\), giving a vector proportional to \((1,-1,2)\).
hints: Orthogonalize first, then normalize.

questions.question: Find an orthonormal basis for the plane.
solutionSteps: Choose two independent vectors in the plane, such as \((1,-1,0)\) and \((1,1,-2)\).
hints: Pick vectors perpendicular to the normal \((1,1,1)\).

questions.question: Find the least-squares solution.
solutionSteps: Form the normal equations \(A^TA\hat{\mathbf x}=A^T\mathbf b\).
hints: Use the normal equations.

questions.question: Determine whether \(Q\) is orthogonal and interpret its geometric effect.
hints: Check \(Q^TQ\) and compare with the standard rotation matrix.
